Setting reaction: The base paste containing hydrosilane-terminated molecules reacts with an accelerator paste containing siloxane oligomers with vinyl end groups and a platinum catalyst. Although no by-product is formed, a secondary reaction leads to hydrogen gas production. That is why it is recommended to wait at least 30 minutes rather than have a pitted cast. [19] 4. Advantages: Excellent dimensional stability - impressions can be stored or posted before casting. Elastic recovery Great accuracy Short setting time Good tear resistance Automix available Hydrophilized addition silicone has good compatibility with gypsum The impression can be cast multiple times without jeopardizing the details [20] 5. Disadvantages: Hydrophobic - the impression area must be dry to prevent inaccuracies in the impression [21] The sulfur present in the latex gloves and rubber dam may interfere with the polymerization of the base and catalyst. Hydrogen gas may lead to pitting in the cast.

Agar has been used for making subgingival crown preparation impressions when it is not easy to keep the area dry. Dimensional stability: As this is a hydrocolloid (water-based), it may lead to deformation due to imbibition (absorption of water ) or syneresis (exudation of fluids) if the impression is not poured immediately. It has an optimum flow to record fine details of hard and soft tissues. Compatibility issues with gypsum arise because borax interferes with gypsum setting; therefore, a gypsum hardener is added. 5. Agar Agar is another hydrophilic colloid impression material like alginate, but it is reversible. It is the first elastic impression material to be used in dentistry. 1. Composition: Chemically, agar is a sulfuric ester of the galactan complex, and agar is available as a tray or syringe material. retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite are influenced by factors such as phase composition, crystal size, and synthesis method. Pure hydroxyapatite typically has a bending strength of 38 to 250 MPa, compressive strength of 120 to 150 MPa, and tensile strength of 38 to 300 MPa. The Young modulus ranges from 35 to 120 GPa, depending on the presence of impurities. [19] Meanwhile, the Weibull modulus, ranging from 5 to 18, indicates hydroxyapatite is a brittle material. To enhance its mechanical properties, tough hydroxyapatite can be obtained by incorporating TCP, which increases its flexural strength. In contrast, the flexural strength reaches its minimum when hydroxyapatite contains calcium oxide CaO. [10] [19] Furthermore, the sintering temperature also significantly alters the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite. Higher sintering temperatures lead to increased density, compressive strength, grain size, and torsional strength. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is a hydrocolloid widely used as an impression material in dentistry. It is derived from alginic acid, present in brown algae. It is an irreversible and elastic impression material. [13] See Image. Alginate, Impression Making. 1. Composition: Sodium or potassium alginate (15%) - dissolves in water and forms a viscous sol Calcium sulfate (16%) - reactor Diatomaceous earth (60%) - filler Zinc oxide (4%) - filler Potassium titanium fluoride (3%) - accelerator Sodium phosphate (2%) - retarder Glycol (in dust-free alginates) Pigments and flavoring agents 2. Indications: Diagnostic casts for treatment planning Orthodontic study models Impressions to fabricate partial and complete dentures, and temporomandibular disorder appliances For duplicating models 3. Instruments and armamentarium: flexible rubber bowl, curved spatula, measuring scoop, perforated impression tray, alginate powder, and water.

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dimensional changes from the sintering process of Y-TZP can lead to non-uniform cement space in single copings. The manufacturing process of Y-TZP blanks may influence the sintering shrinkage pattern and, consequently, on the prostheses fit.

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Y-TZP has a high fracture toughness and flexural strength. When a crack initiates on the surface of Y-TZP, the stress concentration at the top of the crack causes the tetragonal crystal to transform into a monoclinic crystal, with associated volumetric expansion. In the vicinity of a propagating crack, the stress-induced transformation leads to compressive stress that shields the crack tip from the applied stress and enhances the fracture toughness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1827
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a therapeutic formulation that incorporates kalcium-glycerolfosfát and sodium hyaluronate as its constituent components. Kalcium-glycerolfosfát provides a calcium‑phosphate source that can support mineralization processes, while sodium hyaluronate contributes viscoelastic and lubricating properties useful for tissue repair and joint function. By combining these two agents, the replacement preparation is designed to enhance clinical outcomes in procedures that require both structural support and improved biomechanical performance.

Preventive and reparative measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” “Trochanterplasty” reshapes the greater trochanter to improve hip biomechanics. Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 154
retrieved_docs.content: pathology or correct malunited fractures, while “Suture of periosteum of femur” supports periosteal integrity for bone healing. Prosthetic and joint‑related interventions involve “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate,” “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure),” and “Trochanterplasty,” which address femoral implant revision, comprehensive knee arthroplasty component exchange, and greater trochanter reshaping to improve hip biomechanics. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the extensive, femur‑centered surgical repertoire for managing growth disturbances, fractures, malunions, deformities, and prosthetic complications, emphasizing the need for individualized operative planning based on anatomical site, underlying pathology, and desired functional outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a medical formulation that incorporates several specific components, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, dibasic potassium phosphate, and sodium hyaluronate. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát contributes calcium and phosphate ions that can support mineral balance, while dibasic potassium phosphate provides additional phosphate and potassium ions to help maintain physiological pH and electrolyte homeostasis. Sodium hyaluronate, a high‑molecular‑weight glycosaminoglycan, offers viscoelastic properties that are valuable for joint lubrication and tissue repair. Together, these ingredients define the composition of the replacement preparation, highlighting its role in delivering essential minerals and lubricating agents for therapeutic use.

Bone‑shaping and length‑altering procedures—“Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer”—modify femoral geometry to treat limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. “Prophylactic treatment of femur” and “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck” aim to prevent pathology or correct malunited fractures, while “Suture of periosteum of femur” supports periosteal integrity for bone healing. retrieved_docs.content: technique across skeletal regions and is grouped with hip‑head replacement and multiple fractures within the osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام) clinical category, reflecting the heightened need for surgical stabilization in patients with reduced bone density. Collectively, this network of femoral‑focused and lower‑extremity surgical options provides clinicians with comprehensive strategies for fracture stabilization, malunion correction, growth disturbance management, limb‑length modification, prosthetic revision, joint reconstruction, and soft‑tissue repair, thereby supporting optimal functional outcomes across a spectrum of orthopedic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 164
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category aggregates a wide array of operative interventions that target the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated joint structures. Direct femoral procedures include internal fixation of the femur (with or without fracture reduction), intramedullary nailing, open reduction of femur fractures (with or without internal fixation), suture of the periosteum of the femur, repair of distal femur malunion, femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and femoral shortening performed with a blade plate. Osteoplastic techniques—osteoplasty of the femur, femoral lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with segment transfer—address limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. Growth‑modulating strategies comprise epiphysiodesis of the distal femur (and proximal tibia or fibula) and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula for correction of genu varus or valgus. Reconstructive

Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

Procedures that modify femoral length or geometry are classified as having a procedural site rather than a direct site and comprise osteoplasty of the femur for lengthening, osteoplasty of the femur for shortening, and combined lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer, which are employed to correct limb‑length discrepancy and angular deformities. Indirect femoral interventions include replacement of a femoral prosthesis using methyl methacrylate, revision of total knee arthroplasty (all components), and prophylactic treatment of the femur; these are performed as part of broader reconstructive or prosthetic strategies rather than primary bone fixation. Growth‑modulating procedures linked to the femur through a “has relationship” connection are epiphyseal arrest (any method, e.g., epiphysiodesis) of the distal femur and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia or fibula, which are used to halt

Osteoplastic techniques on the femur are detailed as **osteoplasty**, **osteoplasty for lengthening**, **osteoplasty for shortening**, and **combined lengthening‑and‑shortening osteoplasty with femoral segment transfer**. Additional reconstructive and prosthetic measures include **prophylactic treatment of the femur**, **replacement of a femoral prosthesis using methyl methacrylate**, **revision of total knee arthroplasty (all components)**, and the Arabic‑named **إعاضة رأس الفخذ (femoral‑head reconstruction)**.
